questions.question: What does Git do during a fast-forward merge?
options: Creates a new merge commit that combines both branches
Moves the target branch pointer forward to the tip of the source branch
Rebases all commits from the source branch onto the target branch
Copies the files from the source branch into the target branch working directory only
questions.answer: Moves the target branch pointer forward to the tip of the source branch

questions.question: When does a fast-forward merge occur?
options: When both branches have new commits since they diverged
When the target branch has not received any new commits since the source branch was created
When there are merge conflicts between the two branches
When the source branch has been rebased onto the target branch
questions.answer: When the target branch has not received any new commits since the source branch was created

questions.question: Is a merge commit created during a fast-forward merge?
options: Yes, a merge commit is always created during any merge
Yes, but only if you use the --commit flag
No, the branch pointer simply moves forward and the history stays linear
No, but the commit history is rewritten to include a merge marker
questions.answer: No, the branch pointer simply moves forward and the history stays linear

questions.question: What kind of history does a fast-forward merge produce?
options: A branching history with a merge node
A linear (straight-line) history
A circular history that loops back to the branch point
A history with orphaned commits
questions.answer: A linear (straight-line) history

questions.question: When performing a merge, which branch should you be on (checked out)?
options: The feature branch you want to merge from
The branch you want to merge into (e.g., main)
Either branch; Git will figure it out automatically
A temporary merge branch created for the operation
questions.answer: The branch you want to merge into (e.g., main)

questions.question: What command merges a branch called 'add-homepage' into the current branch?
options: git merge --into add-homepage
git merge add-homepage
git branch merge add-homepage
git switch --merge add-homepage
questions.answer: git merge add-homepage

questions.question: After a fast-forward merge, what is the relationship between main and the feature branch pointer?
options: main is one commit behind the feature branch
main and the feature branch point to the same commit
main is one commit ahead of the feature branch
They point to different commits on separate lines of history
questions.answer: main and the feature branch point to the same commit

questions.question: What does 'git branch -d add-homepage' do?
options: Force-deletes the branch even if it has unmerged commits
Deletes the branch only if all its commits have been merged into the current branch
Deletes the branch and all of its commits from history
Deletes the branch and reverts all changes it introduced
questions.answer: Deletes the branch only if all its commits have been merged into the current branch

questions.question: Why is it safe to delete a feature branch after merging it?
options: Git automatically backs up deleted branches in the reflog forever
The commits from the branch are now part of the target branch, so nothing is lost
Deleting a branch never removes commits regardless of merge status
Git prevents deletion of any branch that has ever had commits
questions.answer: The commits from the branch are now part of the target branch, so nothing is lost

questions.question: What is a three-way merge?
options: A merge that involves exactly three commits from the source branch
A merge that uses three branches simultaneously
A merge where both branches have diverged with new commits, and Git creates a merge commit to combine them
A merge that requires three manual conflict resolutions
questions.answer: A merge where both branches have diverged with new commits, and Git creates a merge commit to combine them

questions.question: What causes Git to perform a three-way merge instead of a fast-forward merge?
options: The source branch has more than two commits
Both branches have new commits that the other branch does not have
The branches modify the same file
The user passes the --three-way flag to git merge
questions.answer: Both branches have new commits that the other branch does not have

questions.question: What is a merge commit?
options: The first commit on a new branch
A special commit created by Git that has two parent commits and combines both branches
A commit that deletes the source branch after merging
A commit that only contains metadata about the merge, not actual file changes
questions.answer: A special commit created by Git that has two parent commits and combines both branches

questions.question: During a three-way merge, what happens when Git opens your editor?
options: Git is asking you to resolve a conflict manually
Git is prompting you to write or accept the merge commit message
Git is showing you a diff of the changes being merged
Git is asking you to confirm which files to include in the merge
questions.answer: Git is prompting you to write or accept the merge commit message

questions.question: What command visualizes the branch history as a graph in the terminal?
options: git log --oneline --all --graph
git status --graph --all
git branch --show-graph
git diff --graph --all
questions.answer: git log --oneline --all --graph

questions.question: In the three-way merge example, what are the 'three ways' that Git uses?
options: The three most recent commits on the target branch
The common ancestor commit, the tip of the source branch, and the tip of the target branch
The working directory, the staging area, and the repository
The local branch, the remote branch, and the merge branch
questions.answer: The common ancestor commit, the tip of the source branch, and the tip of the target branch

questions.question: What command switches to the main branch before performing a merge?
options: git checkout merge main
git switch main
git branch main --activate
git move main
questions.answer: git switch main

questions.question: After a three-way merge, what does the branch graph look like?
options: A straight line with no branching
Two parallel lines that never connect
A diamond or fork shape where branches diverge and then rejoin at the merge commit
A single branch with duplicated commits
questions.answer: A diamond or fork shape where branches diverge and then rejoin at the merge commit

questions.question: What does 'git switch -c add-about' do?
options: Switches to an existing branch named add-about
Creates a new branch named add-about and switches to it
Commits changes with the message 'add-about'
Creates a new branch but stays on the current branch
questions.answer: Creates a new branch named add-about and switches to it

questions.question: When merging two feature branches sequentially into main, what type of merge is the first one likely to be?
options: Always a three-way merge
A fast-forward merge, since main has not diverged from the first feature branch
A recursive merge
A squash merge
questions.answer: A fast-forward merge, since main has not diverged from the first feature branch

questions.question: When merging the second feature branch after the first has already been merged, what type of merge occurs?
options: A fast-forward merge, since both branches start from the same point
A three-way merge, because main now has commits the second branch does not
A rebase, since the second branch needs to be updated
No merge is needed because the second branch is already up to date
questions.answer: A three-way merge, because main now has commits the second branch does not

questions.question: After merging both feature-nav and feature-contact into main, what does 'ls' show?
options: Only the files from the last merged branch
Only the files that were on main before any merges
All files from main and both feature branches combined
A MERGE_RESULT file listing all merged content
questions.answer: All files from main and both feature branches combined

questions.question: What does 'git branch' (with no arguments) display after all feature branches are deleted?
options: A list of all branches that ever existed, including deleted ones
Only the remaining branches, such as main
An error because there are no feature branches
The full commit history of the repository
questions.answer: Only the remaining branches, such as main

questions.question: In the fast-forward merge diagram, commit D is the tip of the feature branch. After merging, where does main point?
options: Commit B, where it was before the merge
Commit C, the first commit on the feature branch
Commit D, the tip of the feature branch
A new merge commit M created after D
questions.answer: Commit D, the tip of the feature branch

questions.question: What is the purpose of running 'git log --oneline --all --graph' after each merge?
options: To verify that the merge introduced no bugs
To see the branch structure and confirm the merge was performed correctly
To automatically delete branches that have been merged
To push the merged changes to a remote repository
questions.answer: To see the branch structure and confirm the merge was performed correctly

questions.question: In the three-way merge diagram, what does the commit labeled 'M' represent?
options: The first commit on the main branch
A commit that reverts the feature branch changes
The merge commit that joins both branches back together
A marker commit that Git uses internally but is not visible in the log
questions.answer: The merge commit that joins both branches back together
